myths.Agent_1:
Long before the sky was blue, it was a shimmering expanse of silver. The world below was illuminated in a perpetual, cold twilight. In this silver age lived Aethelgard, a giant woven from the roots of the first mountains. Aethelgard loved the earth but grew tired of the endless, colorless gleam above.

One day, he climbed the tallest peak, carrying a hollowed-out boulder filled with the crushing weight of the deep ocean. As he reached the summit, the silver sky arched just out of reach. With a mighty roar, Aethelgard hurled the ocean-filled boulder upward. It struck the silver dome, shattering it into a million fragments. The fragments fell as the first shooting stars.

The ocean water spilled across the empty void, dyeing the sky a brilliant, endless blue. But the water also brought the tides. The sky began to ebb and flow, creating day and night. The sun, a golden pearl from the ocean's depths, was swept up in these celestial currents.

To this day, when the sky turns gray and rain falls, it is simply the ocean above whispering to the ocean below, remembering the giant who gave the sky its color.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, the sky was a smooth, unbroken canvas of obsidian, and the Moon was a flawless pearl, proud and vain. She boasted that her light was purer than the Sun’s, blinding the creatures of the night. 

The Sun, weary of her arrogance, challenged her to a game of cosmic stones. If the Moon won, she would rule the day; if the Sun won, she would bear the marks of her defeat forever. 

They played across the Milky Way. The Sun, wise and ancient, threw meteors with precision. The Moon, distracted by her own reflection in the Earth's oceans, stumbled. One by one, the Sun's burning stones struck her pristine surface. She cried out as her perfect face was marred with deep, shadowy pits. 

When the game ended, the Moon wept, hiding her scarred face in the shadows. To this day, she only reveals herself in phases, ashamed of the craters that dot her once-flawless skin. Her light is no longer blinding, but a soft, melancholy glow, a reminder to all the heavens that vanity brings ruin, and even the brightest celestial bodies can fall from grace. Now, mortals gaze up with wonder, unaware of the wager that scarred her.

Ylva won the contest, and as her prize, she forever paints the sky with her breath. To this day, the clouds we see are the remnants of Aethelgard's heavy ocean-stones, trapped and shaped by Ylva's eternal winds, dancing across the endless blue canvas in a never-ending game between earth and air.

Myth: When the world was new, the golden Sun shone so fiercely it threatened to scorch the earth to ash. Aethelgard, the giant of the roots and mountains, wept for the drying rivers. Desperate to protect the land, he plunged his massive hands into the deep ocean, violently forging dark, heavy anvils of water. He hurled these massive rain-stones into the sky, creating the first dark storm clouds to block the blazing light.

But his sister, Ylva, the spirit of the restless winds, reveled in the Sun’s fierce heat. Angry at her brother’s interference, she drew in a mighty breath and unleashed a barrage of sharp, freezing gales to tear Aethelgard’s dark clouds apart. 

When Ylva’s piercing winds struck Aethelgard’s dense ocean-anvils, the sheer force of their collision sparked brilliant, jagged cracks of fire across the darkened sky—the very first lightning. The deafening roar of their battle echoed below as thunder. 

To this day, whenever the earth grows too hot, Aethelgard still throws his heavy ocean-stones to shield the ground. But Ylva always strikes back, and the resulting thunderstorms are a reminder of the eternal clash between the protective earth and the rebellious wind.

The Moon, scarred and banished to the darkness, sought to reclaim her lost waters. She reached down with invisible, pulling hands, trying to drag the oceans back into the sky—a struggle mortals now call the tides.

Seeing the Moon's desperate grasp, the Sun took pity on her sorrow. To appease his defeated rival, the Sun shone his fiercest heat upon the ocean surfaces. The warm waters transformed into weightless spirits of vapor, rising into the heavens as drifting clouds. 

At night, these clouds would gather around the Moon, clothing her scarred face in soft, silver veils to hide her craters from the world. However, the clouds were still bound to the earth's memory. Whenever they could no longer bear the weight of their longing, they wept, falling back to the ground as rain. 

Thus, an endless cycle was born. The Sun raises the water to comfort the Moon, and the clouds weep to nourish the earth below. To this day, the clouds dance forever between the Sun's golden warmth and the Moon's melancholy gaze.

Though the Moon’s relentless winter winds eventually strip the branches bare, the fiery leaves fall to the ground, creating a thick, warm blanket that protects the delicate roots below from the deep freeze. Thus, autumn was born—a fleeting, beautiful truce where the trees wear the Sun's armor to brave the Moon’s cold.

Myth: When the Sun gifted his golden and crimson fire to the forests, allowing the great oaks and maples to shed their leaves and slumber safely beneath the snow, most of the woods accepted this seasonal retreat. They surrendered their crowns to the Moon’s icy winter winds in exchange for peaceful rest.

But the proud pines and ancient firs refused to bow to the Moon’s bitter wrath. They rejected the Sun’s fleeting autumn armor, unwilling to stand bare and defenseless for half the year. Instead, they drew the earth's deepest, most resilient green magic up into their branches and compressed their broad, fragile leaves into sharp, hardened needles. 

When the Moon unleashed her freezing gales, the needles did not catch the heavy snow; they pierced right through the frost. The sap within their trunks thickened into a fragrant, stubborn resin that burned like slow fire, keeping their hearts warm even as the world around them froze solid. 

Furious, the Moon battered them with her harshest blizzards, but the evergreens stood unyielding. To this day, they remain awake and vibrantly green through the darkest winters, silent sentinels proving that life can endure even the coldest, most unforgiving nights.
